title: The hidden cost of professionalising what used to be neighbourly
summary: Argue that the steady professionalisation of work that was once done informally — care, food, repair, watching a neighbour's child — solves some problems while quietly creating others that the professional version cannot address.
topic: What is lost when informal mutual aid becomes a professional service
prompt: Write a 600–800-word essay arguing that the professionalisation of formerly informal work is not merely a market shift but a moral one — with real gains and real, distributed losses. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'Granted… Even so…' concession. Set examples in named cities. Avoid the language of grassroots-romance and 'the way things used to be'.
hintStarters: Only rarely does the substitution of a professional for a neighbour leave the underlying relationship unchanged…
What the professional service supplies in reliability it tends to remove in…
Granted, the older arrangements relied on a free labour that almost always fell on women. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lao động mang định kiến giới
sự tiêu chuẩn hoá dịch vụ
sự loãng đi của các mối quan hệ xã hội
sự mất kỹ năng của hộ gia đình
sự biến mất của món nợ
sổ cái của những ân huệ
ngữ pháp đạo đức của sự giúp đỡ
hộ gia đình bị nguyên tử hoá
sự tương hỗ đã ổn định

sampleText:
Only rarely does the substitution of a professional for a neighbour leave the underlying relationship unchanged. When a Hanoi mother who used to leave her two-year-old with the woman across the corridor begins, instead, to pay a registered childcare service, what changes is not only who watches the child. The corridor woman, freed from the unspoken duty, no longer expects to be invited to weddings; the mother, freed from the reciprocal obligation to watch the other woman's mother on Sunday mornings, no longer registers her own household as embedded in a small web of debts. What had been a relationship has become, on both sides, an absence — managed, paid for, and quieter than before.

No one mourns the older arrangements without qualification. They were demanding, often unfair, and tended to extract their costs from people — usually women, usually older — whose alternatives were not abundant. The corridor woman was, in many cases, not a willing volunteer but an unpaid carer with few options. A paid service, however imperfect, has the moral virtue of being explicitly compensated, contractually limited, and refusable in a way the informal favour rarely was. To prefer the older arrangement on principle is to be insensitive to who actually bore its costs.

What the professional service supplies in reliability it tends to remove in something for which we do not have a single word. Call it the moral grammar of help: the small accumulation of unsettled debts between households, where my watching your child today incurs a quiet expectation that your son will help me with the boxes when I move, and where neither of us would ever speak of it because to speak of it would falsify it. This grammar produces relationships; the cash relation produces transactions. The two can coexist, but they do not substitute for each other, and the latter does not, on its own, generate the former.

It is the displacement of one moral economy by another, not the simple addition of new services, that gives professionalisation its particular character. A neighbourhood in Lisbon where every formerly informal exchange has been re-routed through a paid intermediary is not a richer neighbourhood with extra services available; it is a neighbourhood in which the original web has been dissolved and replaced by a series of vertical relations with companies. The neighbours, no longer indebted to one another, no longer have occasions to meet that arise from need. They have, instead, occasions that arise from leisure — and leisure, it turns out, is a thinner glue.

There is also a quieter cost in skill. A household that has paid someone, for years, to do its small repairs, its food preparation, its child-minding, accumulates a deskilling that becomes hard to reverse. When the paid service is no longer affordable — because income contracts, because the company exits, because the worker has moved — the household no longer knows how to do the things it once knew, and no longer has neighbours who remember either. This is not a moralising point about laziness. It is an observation about how cultures of capability are sustained: by ordinary, slightly inconvenient practice, distributed across many households, none of which can produce the practice on their own.

Granted, the older arrangements relied on a free labour that almost always fell on women, and any honest account of professionalisation must record that as a real gain when paid alternatives became available. Even so, the gain has not been distributed in the way the policy literature sometimes suggests. The people freed by paid services are, mostly, the people who could afford to pay; the people whose labour now appears in the accounts are, mostly, the same gendered group, transferred from an unpaid to a low-paid position. The professionalisation has not abolished the older asymmetry. It has, in many cases, marketised it.

None of this argues against paid services or for a return to arrangements no one chose. The argument is narrower: that what we gain in efficiency we pay for in something that does not appear on the bill — a thinning of the small mutualities on which neighbourhoods quietly run. To notice that is not to prescribe a return. It is to keep open the question of what, in our particular street, in our particular household, we might still be able to do for one another without an invoice.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Negative-fronted inversion to open with a precise generalisation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở đầu 'Only rarely does the substitution of a professional for a neighbour leave the underlying relationship unchanged' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ dài 'the substitution of a professional for a neighbour' sau cụm phủ định 'Only rarely'. Đảo ngữ ở đây có tác dụng kép: trang trọng hoá lập luận và đặt mệnh đề chính ('leave the underlying relationship unchanged') ở vị trí nhấn mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Only rarely does the substitution of a professional for a neighbour leave the underlying relationship unchanged' inverts 'does' before a long subject. The inversion formalises the claim and puts the predicate 'leave the underlying relationship unchanged' in the position of greatest weight — the focus of the essay's argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to name the real character of the change
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the displacement of one moral economy by another, not the simple addition of new services, that gives professionalisation its particular character' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z' để bác bỏ cách hiểu thông thường (thêm dịch vụ mới) và đề xuất cách hiểu đúng (sự dịch chuyển của một nền kinh tế đạo đức bởi một nền kinh tế khác).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the displacement of one moral economy by another, not the simple addition of new services, that gives professionalisation its particular character' uses 'It is X, not Y, that Z' to reject the addition-of-services framing and propose displacement as the right description. The cleft enacts the essay's reframing move.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chỉ hiếm khi việc thay thế một người chuyên nghiệp cho một người hàng xóm để lại mối quan hệ nền tảng không thay đổi. Khi một bà mẹ ở Hà Nội từng để đứa con hai tuổi cho người phụ nữ ngang hành lang bắt đầu, thay vào đó, trả tiền cho một dịch vụ trông trẻ đã đăng ký, điều thay đổi không chỉ là ai trông đứa trẻ. Người phụ nữ ở hành lang, được giải phóng khỏi nghĩa vụ không nói ra, không còn mong đợi được mời đến đám cưới; bà mẹ, được giải phóng khỏi nghĩa vụ tương hỗ trông mẹ của người phụ nữ kia vào sáng Chủ nhật, không còn ghi nhận hộ gia đình của chính mình như được nhúng vào một mạng nhỏ các món nợ.

Không ai tiếc nuối các sắp xếp cũ mà không có điều kiện. Chúng đòi hỏi, thường bất công, và có xu hướng trích chi phí từ những người — thường là phụ nữ, thường lớn tuổi — mà lựa chọn không dồi dào. Một dịch vụ trả tiền, dù không hoàn hảo, có đức tính đạo đức là được bồi thường rõ ràng, được giới hạn bằng hợp đồng, và có thể từ chối theo cách mà ân huệ không chính thức hiếm khi có.

Những gì dịch vụ chuyên nghiệp cung cấp về độ tin cậy nó có xu hướng loại bỏ ở điều mà chúng ta không có một từ duy nhất nào. Gọi nó là ngữ pháp đạo đức của sự giúp đỡ: sự tích lũy nhỏ của các món nợ chưa giải quyết giữa các hộ gia đình, nơi tôi trông con bạn hôm nay tạo ra một kỳ vọng âm thầm rằng con trai bạn sẽ giúp tôi với các thùng khi tôi chuyển nhà, và nơi không ai trong chúng tôi sẽ bao giờ nói về nó vì nói về nó sẽ làm giả nó. Ngữ pháp này tạo ra các mối quan hệ; quan hệ tiền mặt tạo ra các giao dịch.

Chính sự dịch chuyển của một nền kinh tế đạo đức bởi một nền kinh tế khác, không phải sự thêm vào đơn giản các dịch vụ mới, mới mang lại cho sự chuyên nghiệp hoá đặc tính riêng biệt. Một khu phố ở Lisbon nơi mọi trao đổi không chính thức đã được định tuyến lại qua một trung gian trả tiền không phải là một khu phố giàu có hơn với các dịch vụ bổ sung; đó là một khu phố trong đó mạng gốc đã bị giải thể và thay thế bởi một loạt các quan hệ dọc với các công ty. Các hàng xóm, không còn nợ nhau, không còn dịp gặp nhau phát sinh từ nhu cầu. Thay vào đó, họ có những dịp phát sinh từ thời gian rảnh — và thời gian rảnh, hoá ra, là chất keo mỏng hơn.

Cũng có một chi phí trầm lặng hơn về kỹ năng. Một hộ gia đình đã trả ai đó, trong nhiều năm, để làm các sửa chữa nhỏ, chuẩn bị thức ăn, trông trẻ, tích lũy một sự mất kỹ năng trở nên khó đảo ngược. Khi dịch vụ trả tiền không còn đủ khả năng — vì thu nhập co lại, vì công ty rút lui, vì người lao động đã chuyển đi — hộ gia đình không còn biết cách làm những điều nó từng biết, và không còn hàng xóm nào nhớ.

Cứ cho là các sắp xếp cũ dựa vào lao động miễn phí mà hầu như luôn rơi vào phụ nữ, và bất kỳ tường thuật trung thực nào về sự chuyên nghiệp hoá phải ghi nhận điều đó như một lợi ích thực sự khi các lựa chọn trả tiền trở nên khả dụng. Dù vậy, lợi ích đã không được phân phối theo cách mà tài liệu chính sách đôi khi gợi ý. Những người được giải phóng bởi các dịch vụ trả tiền là, hầu hết, những người có thể chi trả; những người mà lao động giờ xuất hiện trong các tài khoản là, hầu hết, cùng nhóm giới tính, được chuyển từ vị trí không lương sang vị trí lương thấp. Sự chuyên nghiệp hoá đã không xoá bỏ sự bất đối xứng cũ. Nó, trong nhiều trường hợp, đã thị trường hoá nó.

Không có gì ở đây tranh luận chống lại các dịch vụ trả tiền hoặc cho việc quay trở lại các sắp xếp mà không ai chọn. Lập luận hẹp hơn: rằng những gì chúng ta thu được về hiệu quả chúng ta trả bằng điều gì đó không xuất hiện trên hoá đơn — sự loãng đi của những sự tương hỗ nhỏ mà các khu phố âm thầm vận hành. Nhận ra điều đó không phải là kê đơn một sự quay trở lại. Đó là giữ mở câu hỏi về những gì, trên con phố cụ thể của chúng ta, trong hộ gia đình cụ thể của chúng ta, chúng ta vẫn có thể làm cho nhau mà không cần hoá đơn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after 'Only rarely'?
options: Only rarely the substitution of a professional leaves the relationship unchanged.
Only rarely does the substitution of a professional for a neighbour leave the underlying relationship unchanged.
The substitution of a professional only rarely leaves the underlying relationship unchanged.
Only rarely is the substitution of a professional leave the relationship unchanged.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Only rarely', the auxiliary 'does' precedes the subject, and the lexical verb returns to base form ('leave', not 'leaves').
mcQuestions.hint: Negative fronting + do-support; lexical verb in base form.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central observation about the Hanoi mother's switch?
options: She regrets paying for childcare
What changes is not only who watches the child — the relationship with the corridor woman, and the household's place in a small web of debts, also changes
Paid childcare is more expensive than the informal arrangement
The corridor woman becomes the mother's enemy
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's opening example argues that paying restructures the broader web of obligations, not just the immediate task — the relationship becomes 'an absence — managed, paid for, and quieter than before.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what changes besides the immediate transaction.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's view of the older informal arrangements?
options: Romantic and unambiguously good
Demanding, often unfair, and extractive — but with one moral virtue absent from the paid version: deeply embedded reciprocity
Perfect and unimprovable
Identical to modern paid services
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay refuses nostalgia: the older arrangements were extractive, often gendered, and unfair to those who bore their costs. The case for them rests on what they produced socially, not on their moral perfection.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the second paragraph's qualification.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the moral grammar of help'?
options: A formal code of ethics for caregivers
The small accumulation of unsettled debts between households, sustained by reciprocity that is never explicitly named because naming it would falsify it
A grammar textbook published by a religious institution
The legal duties of citizens to one another
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay coins 'the moral grammar of help' to name the implicit, unspoken structure of reciprocal obligation — the very thing the cash relation cannot reproduce.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the paragraph where the term is introduced.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to characterise professionalisation?
options: Professionalisation adds new services to neighbourhoods.
It is the displacement of one moral economy by another, not the simple addition of new services, that gives professionalisation its particular character.
Both displacement and addition characterise professionalisation.
Professionalisation replaces all services with new ones.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that Z' is a cleft that rejects the addition-of-services framing and substitutes displacement of one moral economy by another — a C1 structure enacting the essay's reframing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is ... not ... that ...' construction.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay describe as a 'thinner glue' for neighbourhoods?
options: Government policy
Leisure — encounters arising from spare time rather than from need
Paid municipal services
Online communication
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay observes that when the web of need is dissolved, neighbours meet through leisure, which is a thinner glue than the reciprocity of dependence.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about deskilling?
options: It is an irrelevant side-issue

It is a quieter cost: households lose ordinary capabilities that were sustained by distributed informal practice, and those capabilities are hard to reassemble when the paid service is no longer affordable
It only affects elderly people
It is solved by adult-education classes
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes deskilling as a slow loss of household capability — repair, food preparation, child-minding — that follows when these tasks are outsourced over years.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the paragraph about cultures of capability.

options: Even so, women bore the unpaid work. Granted, professionalisation has not redistributed it.

Granted, the older arrangements relied on a free labour that almost always fell on women, and any honest account must record that. Even so, the gain has not been distributed as policy literature sometimes suggests.
Granted and even so, the gain has been distributed and not distributed.
Granted that policy is wrong. Even so, women still work.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted' concedes that the older arrangements were gendered; 'Even so' qualifies the policy story about how the gain is distributed. Concession then pivot.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say has happened to the gendered asymmetry of care work?
options: It has been abolished by paid services
It has been marketised rather than abolished — the same gendered group is now in a low-paid position rather than an unpaid one
It has been reversed — men now do most care work
It is no longer a relevant concern
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay observes that paid services have transferred women from unpaid to low-paid care work — a real improvement, but a marketisation of asymmetry rather than its disappearance.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the Granted/Even so paragraph for the gendered argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about leisure as a basis for neighbourhood connection?
options: Leisure is the best basis because it is freely chosen
Leisure produces thinner ties than reciprocal need — meeting from spare time is less binding than meeting from interdependence
Leisure is identical to need-based reciprocity
Leisure has no role in neighbourhood relations
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay distinguishes leisure-based meetings from need-based reciprocity: 'leisure, it turns out, is a thinner glue.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'thinner glue' and what it contrasts with.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay characterise the cash relation?
options: It produces relationships in the same way reciprocal obligation does
It produces transactions, which can coexist with relationships but do not, on their own, generate them
It is morally inferior in every respect
It is morally superior because it is voluntary
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay carefully distinguishes: the moral grammar of help produces relationships, the cash relation produces transactions. They can coexist; the latter does not substitute.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say it is NOT arguing for?
options: More attention to neighbourhood ties
Awareness of what professionalisation costs
A return to arrangements no one chose, or a rejection of paid services
Recognition of the moral grammar of help
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing paragraph explicitly disclaims: 'None of this argues against paid services or for a return to arrangements no one chose.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's opening.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'cultures of capability are sustained by ordinary, slightly inconvenient practice'?
options: Capability requires formal training programmes
Skills survive in a community because many households practice them slightly more often than is strictly convenient — collective competence depends on distributed inconvenience
Practice is only sustainable if it is convenient
Inconvenience is a virtue in itself
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's argument is that a culture of skill is a distributed property — many households doing slightly inconvenient work — and that when each household outsources, the collective skill base erodes.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the deskilling paragraph for how skills are sustained.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's closing question ask?
options: Whether we should refuse all paid services
What, in our particular street and household, we might still be able to do for one another without an invoice
Whether all unpaid labour should be compensated
Whether professionalisation is finished
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing turns the argument into a question for the reader's actual situation: not abstract policy, but what specifically might be sustained without a bill.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best summarises the essay's view?
options: Paid services are always worse than informal arrangements
Informal arrangements are always worse than paid services
Professionalisation is a moral shift that produces real gains and real, distributed losses — losses that do not appear on the bill but that change the neighbourhood
Neighbourhoods do not need either paid services or informal arrangements
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay holds both observations: paid services solve real problems (unfairness, gendered extraction) but produce real losses (thinning ties, deskilling) that markets do not record.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the overall structure of the argument.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of something that used to be done informally in your family, building, or neighbourhood — and is now done by a paid service. What has been gained? What, if anything, has been lost that does not appear on the bill? Is the trade worth it on the specific terms of your case?
